Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3701558

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Image fusion combines images from multiple sources to produce detailed results, but real-world situations often include unpaired samples with missing data due to issues like data privacy or camera differences. This challenge underscores the need to reconstruct unpaired samples and achieve effective fusion, improving decision-making in applications where data is incomplete. In this paper, we propose E2M-Fusion, the first End-to-end Multi-level model for multimodal image Fusion with missing data, which can bring unpaired sample reconstruction and image fusion into an end-to-end framework. E2M-Fusion operates at three levels: (1) a Supported Images Retrieval (SIR) strategy at the data level, leveraging paired samples and a vision-language model to retrieve reliable references for reconstruction; (2) a Fourier Contrastive Diffusion (FCD) model at the representation level, combining Fourier Transform, contrastive learning, and diffusion techniques for high-quality unpaired sample reconstruction; and (3) an Adaptive Labeling Fusion (ALF) model at the decision level, utilizing multi-task evaluation and iterative optimization to generate robust labels for fusion. Unlike existing methods limited to paired data, E2M-Fusion can address modality missing and fuses images in a unified framework. Extensive experiments on public datasets (M3FD, TNO, and LLVIP) demonstrate E2M Fusion can effectively handle the task of fusing unpaired images in real-world scenarios.

item.title: A Multi-Task Framework for Interpreting Pavement Subsurface Distress from GPR Data with Multimodal Vision-Language Model
item.dc:creator: Bingxin Yang
Chen Guo
Keran Hao
Zhao Jin
Zhili He
Dou Tian
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: In recent years, Deep Learning (DL) has become an effective tool for analyzing Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) data. It enables the rapid identification of pavement subsurface distress. However, traditional single-task DL models often fail to provide comprehensive and effective interpretation in real engineering scenarios. To address this problem, this paper proposes a multi-task interpretation framework based on the multimodal Vision-Language model PaliGemma, along with an efficient annotation method. The framework enables a streamlined and intelligent GPR interpretation pipeline that sequentially integrates object detection, semantic segmentation, and image description. To improve the annotation efficiency, we introduce an Automatic Feature-Sensitive Semantic Labeling (AFSSL) algorithm for generating segmentation labels. We also design a structured text template to support the automatic generation of standard-compliant distress descriptions. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to that of specialized single-task models across object detection, semantic segmentation, and image description tasks. Moreover, the proposed framework demonstrates strong domain-adaptation capability while maintaining competitive performance. It also produces a comprehensive interpretation report in about 46 seconds on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU. Overall, the proposed framework offers an efficient and practical solution for intelligent GPR interpretation, providing valuable support for pavement condition evaluation and informed decision-making. Published: 2026-06-08T19:51:37+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.778 (must_read)

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1109/tgrs.2026.3701293

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

In recent years, Deep Learning (DL) has become an effective tool for analyzing Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) data. It enables the rapid identification of pavement subsurface distress. However, traditional single-task DL models often fail to provide comprehensive and effective interpretation in real engineering scenarios. To address this problem, this paper proposes a multi-task interpretation framework based on the multimodal Vision-Language model PaliGemma, along with an efficient annotation method. The framework enables a streamlined and intelligent GPR interpretation pipeline that sequentially integrates object detection, semantic segmentation, and image description. To improve the annotation efficiency, we introduce an Automatic Feature-Sensitive Semantic Labeling (AFSSL) algorithm for generating segmentation labels. We also design a structured text template to support the automatic generation of standard-compliant distress descriptions. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves accuracy comparable to that of specialized single-task models across object detection, semantic segmentation, and image description tasks. Moreover, the proposed framework demonstrates strong domain-adaptation capability while maintaining competitive performance. It also produces a comprehensive interpretation report in about 46 seconds on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU. Overall, the proposed framework offers an efficient and practical solution for intelligent GPR interpretation, providing valuable support for pavement condition evaluation and informed decision-making.

item.title: Universal Facial Landmark Detection by Landmark-Clustering Relation-Reasoning Transformer
item.dc:creator: Jun Wan
Yuanzhi Yao
Jiaxing Huang
Xiaoying Ding
Lefei Zhang
Yongsheng Gao
Dacheng Tao
item.description: Current facial landmark detection methods typically train separate models for individual datasets, limiting their ability to fully leverage labeled information across datasets and reducing the landmark detection accuracy. Additionally, these models continue to face challenges with large poses and partial occlusions, as they struggle to accurately infer facial structures under these conditions. To tackle these challenges, this paper proposes a universal facial landmark detection framework called Landmark-Clustering Relation-Reasoning Transformer (LCRRT), which enables an all-in-one model that allows testing across different datasets without changing any parameters. LCRRT explores the dependencies between image tokens by a Transformer architecture, and models facial landmark detection as a progressively optimized landmark coordinate prediction process. To achieve facial landmark detection across datasets, a novel Hierarchical Facial Region Clustering (HFRC) model is designed to yield region tokens through a parameterized clustering method for learning a universal facial structure. Additionally, a Landmark Relation Reasoning (LRR) loss is introduced to guide the clustering process to further improve the universal feature learning. This is the first study to explore an all-in-one facial landmark detection model by modeling a universal facial structure. Extensive experimental results on popular benchmark datasets demonstrate that our LCRRT achieves comparable performance with the state-of-the-art methods. The code is available at https://github.com/bleucon/LCRRT . Published: 2026-06-08T03:12:53+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jun Wan; Yuanzhi Yao; Jiaxing Huang; Xiaoying Ding; Lefei Zhang; Yongsheng Gao; Dacheng Tao

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02881-5

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

Current facial landmark detection methods typically train separate models for individual datasets, limiting their ability to fully leverage labeled information across datasets and reducing the landmark detection accuracy. Additionally, these models continue to face challenges with large poses and partial occlusions, as they struggle to accurately infer facial structures under these conditions. To tackle these challenges, this paper proposes a universal facial landmark detection framework called Landmark-Clustering Relation-Reasoning Transformer (LCRRT), which enables an all-in-one model that allows testing across different datasets without changing any parameters. LCRRT explores the dependencies between image tokens by a Transformer architecture, and models facial landmark detection as a progressively optimized landmark coordinate prediction process. To achieve facial landmark detection across datasets, a novel Hierarchical Facial Region Clustering (HFRC) model is designed to yield region tokens through a parameterized clustering method for learning a universal facial structure. Additionally, a Landmark Relation Reasoning (LRR) loss is introduced to guide the clustering process to further improve the universal feature learning. This is the first study to explore an all-in-one facial landmark detection model by modeling a universal facial structure. Extensive experimental results on popular benchmark datasets demonstrate that our LCRRT achieves comparable performance with the state-of-the-art methods. The code is available at https://github.com/bleucon/LCRRT .

item.title: Retrieval-augmented diffusion with acoustic priors for high-fidelity sonar image generation
item.dc:creator: Shaocong Yang
Zheng Gu
Hongye Cao
Xiaolong Qi
Ling Lin
Jing Huo
Yang Gao
item.description: High-fidelity sonar image generation is crucial for underwater perception, yet current generative models struggle to capture complex acoustic characteristics. We propose a retrieval-augmented diffusion model incorporating acoustic priors to address this. The method retrieves reference sonar samples using visible-light inputs, encoding them into multi-scale features that capture data-driven acoustic signatures. These priors, combined with RGB semantic features, guide a diffusion process to synthesize physically plausible sonar images. Evaluations on the RGBS50 and Sonar-to-RGB Translation datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in acoustic consistency and structural integrity. Results across metrics like FID, PSNR, and SSIM confirm its superior performance and cross-dataset generalization. In addition, the proposed method demonstrates strong robustness across diverse challenging scenarios, including low-light, low-resolution, and low sonar reflectivity conditions. It also exhibits excellent generalization capability to unseen object categories, and its effectiveness is further validated through superior performance in downstream perception tasks. Published: 2026-06-08T05:41:19+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.776 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shaocong Yang; Zheng Gu; Hongye Cao; Xiaolong Qi; Ling Lin; Jing Huo; Yang Gao

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114165

Relevance: 0.776 (must_read)

High-fidelity sonar image generation is crucial for underwater perception, yet current generative models struggle to capture complex acoustic characteristics. We propose a retrieval-augmented diffusion model incorporating acoustic priors to address this. The method retrieves reference sonar samples using visible-light inputs, encoding them into multi-scale features that capture data-driven acoustic signatures. These priors, combined with RGB semantic features, guide a diffusion process to synthesize physically plausible sonar images. Evaluations on the RGBS50 and Sonar-to-RGB Translation datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in acoustic consistency and structural integrity. Results across metrics like FID, PSNR, and SSIM confirm its superior performance and cross-dataset generalization. In addition, the proposed method demonstrates strong robustness across diverse challenging scenarios, including low-light, low-resolution, and low sonar reflectivity conditions. It also exhibits excellent generalization capability to unseen object categories, and its effectiveness is further validated through superior performance in downstream perception tasks.

item.title: ONE-FOR-ALL: Towards unified zero-shot anomaly detection via adaptive prompt learning
item.dc:creator: Yuqing Zhao
Min Meng
Jigang Wu
item.description: Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) requires detection models trained on auxiliary data to identify anomalies without any training samples in the target dataset. This task is particularly important when data collection is limited by privacy or accessibility concerns, yet highly challenging since the models must generalize across diverse domains where object appearance, abnormal regions, and background features vary significantly. Recently, large pre-trained vision–language models (VLMs) such as CLIP have shown strong zero-shot recognition ability, but their ZSAD performance remains limited because they emphasize object semantics rather than normality/abnormality cues. In this paper, we introduce Adaptive Unified Prompt Anomaly Detection (AUPAD), the first one-for-all ZSAD framework that leverages the zero-shot recognition ability of VLMs through instance-adaptive prompt learning. Unlike prior methods with fixed category-specific prompts, AUPAD generates adaptive prompts by combining common semantic vectors with instance-adaptive tokens, enabling precise modeling of both normal and abnormal states in a category-guided manner. Furthermore, AUPAD incorporates an object-agnostic prompt template and a glocal abnormality loss to capture generic anomaly semantics from auxiliary data, while a Dynamic Region Attention Module (DRAM) alleviates global attention bias and enhances local semantic consistency without modifying the pretrained encoder. Extensive experiments on 17 industrial and medical datasets demonstrate that AUPAD achieves superior performance in detecting and segmenting anomalies across highly diverse semantic categories. Code will be made available at https://github.com/hanli6688/AUPAD . Published: 2026-06-08T16:23:18+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuqing Zhao; Min Meng; Jigang Wu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.114123

Relevance: 0.775 (must_read)

Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) requires detection models trained on auxiliary data to identify anomalies without any training samples in the target dataset. This task is particularly important when data collection is limited by privacy or accessibility concerns, yet highly challenging since the models must generalize across diverse domains where object appearance, abnormal regions, and background features vary significantly. Recently, large pre-trained vision–language models (VLMs) such as CLIP have shown strong zero-shot recognition ability, but their ZSAD performance remains limited because they emphasize object semantics rather than normality/abnormality cues. In this paper, we introduce Adaptive Unified Prompt Anomaly Detection (AUPAD), the first one-for-all ZSAD framework that leverages the zero-shot recognition ability of VLMs through instance-adaptive prompt learning. Unlike prior methods with fixed category-specific prompts, AUPAD generates adaptive prompts by combining common semantic vectors with instance-adaptive tokens, enabling precise modeling of both normal and abnormal states in a category-guided manner. Furthermore, AUPAD incorporates an object-agnostic prompt template and a glocal abnormality loss to capture generic anomaly semantics from auxiliary data, while a Dynamic Region Attention Module (DRAM) alleviates global attention bias and enhances local semantic consistency without modifying the pretrained encoder. Extensive experiments on 17 industrial and medical datasets demonstrate that AUPAD achieves superior performance in detecting and segmenting anomalies across highly diverse semantic categories. Code will be made available at https://github.com/hanli6688/AUPAD .

item.title: Training-Free Zero-Shot Temporal Action Detection with Vision-Language Models
item.dc:creator: Chaolei Han
Hongsong Wang
Jidong Kuang
Lei Zhang
Jie Gui
item.description: Existing zero-shot temporal action detection (ZSTAD) methods predominantly use fully supervised or unsupervised strategies to recognize unseen activities. However, these training-based methods are susceptible to domain shifts and entail high computational costs. Unlike previous works, we present FreeZAD, an training-free method for ZSTAD, which leverages image-pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to directly classify and localize unseen activities within untrimmed videos. We mitigate the need for explicit temporal modeling and reliance on pseudo-label quality by designing the Logarithmic decay weighted Outer-Inner-Contrastive Score (LogOIC) and frequency-based actionness calibration. Furthermore, we introduce a test-time adaptation (TTA) strategy using Prototype-Centric Sampling (PCS) to expand FreeZAD, enabling VLMs to adapt more effectively for ZSTAD. Extensive experiments on the THUMOS14 and ActivityNet-1.3 datasets demonstrate that our training-free method outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods while requiring only 1/13 of the runtime, and the adaptation-enhanced extension further narrows the gap with fully supervised approaches. The code are available at: https://github.com/Chaolei98/FreeZAD. Published: 2026-06-08T19:52:35+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chaolei Han; Hongsong Wang; Jidong Kuang; Lei Zhang; Jie Gui

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3701531

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Existing zero-shot temporal action detection (ZSTAD) methods predominantly use fully supervised or unsupervised strategies to recognize unseen activities. However, these training-based methods are susceptible to domain shifts and entail high computational costs. Unlike previous works, we present FreeZAD, an training-free method for ZSTAD, which leverages image-pretrained vision-language models (VLMs) to directly classify and localize unseen activities within untrimmed videos. We mitigate the need for explicit temporal modeling and reliance on pseudo-label quality by designing the Logarithmic decay weighted Outer-Inner-Contrastive Score (LogOIC) and frequency-based actionness calibration. Furthermore, we introduce a test-time adaptation (TTA) strategy using Prototype-Centric Sampling (PCS) to expand FreeZAD, enabling VLMs to adapt more effectively for ZSTAD. Extensive experiments on the THUMOS14 and ActivityNet-1.3 datasets demonstrate that our training-free method outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised methods while requiring only 1/13 of the runtime, and the adaptation-enhanced extension further narrows the gap with fully supervised approaches. The code are available at: https://github.com/Chaolei98/FreeZAD.

item.title: Unsupervised Online 3D Instance Segmentation with Synthetic Sequences and Dynamic Loss
item.dc:creator: Yifan Zhang
Wei Zhang
Chuangxin He
Zhonghua Miao
Junhui Hou
item.description: Unsupervised online 3D instance segmentation is a fundamental yet challenging task, as it requires maintaining consistent object identities across LiDAR scans without relying on annotated training data. Existing methods, such as UNIT, have made progress in this direction but remain constrained by limited training diversity, rigid temporal sampling, and heavy dependence on noisy pseudo-labels. We propose a new framework that enriches the training distribution through synthetic point cloud sequence generation, enabling greater diversity without relying on manual labels or simulation engines. To better capture temporal dynamics, our method incorporates a flexible sampling strategy that leverages both adjacent and non-adjacent frames, allowing the model to learn from long-range dependencies as well as short-term variations. In addition, a dynamic-weighting loss emphasizes confident and informative samples, guiding the network toward more robust representations. Through extensive experiments on SemanticKITTI, nuScenes, and PandaSet, our method consistently outperforms UNIT and other unsupervised baselines, achieving higher segmentation accuracy and more robust temporal associations. The code will be publicly available at github.com/Eaphan/SFT3D. Published: 2026-06-08T19:52:35+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yifan Zhang; Wei Zhang; Chuangxin He; Zhonghua Miao; Junhui Hou

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia

DOI: 10.1109/tmm.2026.3701167

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Unsupervised online 3D instance segmentation is a fundamental yet challenging task, as it requires maintaining consistent object identities across LiDAR scans without relying on annotated training data. Existing methods, such as UNIT, have made progress in this direction but remain constrained by limited training diversity, rigid temporal sampling, and heavy dependence on noisy pseudo-labels. We propose a new framework that enriches the training distribution through synthetic point cloud sequence generation, enabling greater diversity without relying on manual labels or simulation engines. To better capture temporal dynamics, our method incorporates a flexible sampling strategy that leverages both adjacent and non-adjacent frames, allowing the model to learn from long-range dependencies as well as short-term variations. In addition, a dynamic-weighting loss emphasizes confident and informative samples, guiding the network toward more robust representations. Through extensive experiments on SemanticKITTI, nuScenes, and PandaSet, our method consistently outperforms UNIT and other unsupervised baselines, achieving higher segmentation accuracy and more robust temporal associations. The code will be publicly available at github.com/Eaphan/SFT3D.

item.title: CL-CLIP: CLIP-Based Continual Learning Framework with Cost-Volume Category Decoupling for Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Zihan Liu
Yuguang Yang
Shengjie Su
Jianing Pang
Linlin Yang
Chunyu Xie
Nikolai Yu. Zolotykh
Baochang Zhang
item.description: Continual Object Detection (COD) requires a detector to acquire new categories over time while preserving previously learned ones. This goal is closely related to open-vocabulary detection, since both settings require reasoning over categories that are not fully covered by the annotations available at the current training stage. Recent CLIP-based open-vocabulary detectors have shown strong zero-shot generalization, and frameworks such as F-ViT demonstrate that vision-language pretraining can provide powerful zero-shot detection ability for unseen categories. However, real-world deployments cannot remain purely zero-shot: once these detectors are continually updated on newly introduced categories, they suffer severe catastrophic forgetting and quickly lose their previously calibrated detection ability. We therefore propose CL-CLIP, a CLIP-based COD framework that equips open-vocabulary detectors with better continual learning ability through cost-volume-guided category decoupling. Specifically, following CAT-Seg, we compute a CLIP image-text similarity cost volume, defined as dense category-wise response maps between visual tokens and class text embeddings. This zero-shot spatial prior decomposes shared region features into class-specific pathways, which are then processed by a Multi-Expert RoI head. Extensive experiments on PASCAL VOC and MS-COCO show that CL-CLIP substantially improves the F-ViT baseline under continual fine-tuning and achieves competitive performance with existing continual object detectors, especially in adapting to newly introduced categories while preserving competitive base-class performance. Published: 2026-06-05T07:09:52+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.774 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zihan Liu; Yuguang Yang; Shengjie Su; Jianing Pang; Linlin Yang; Chunyu Xie; Nikolai Yu. Zolotykh; Baochang Zhang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.774 (must_read)

Continual Object Detection (COD) requires a detector to acquire new categories over time while preserving previously learned ones. This goal is closely related to open-vocabulary detection, since both settings require reasoning over categories that are not fully covered by the annotations available at the current training stage. Recent CLIP-based open-vocabulary detectors have shown strong zero-shot generalization, and frameworks such as F-ViT demonstrate that vision-language pretraining can provide powerful zero-shot detection ability for unseen categories. However, real-world deployments cannot remain purely zero-shot: once these detectors are continually updated on newly introduced categories, they suffer severe catastrophic forgetting and quickly lose their previously calibrated detection ability. We therefore propose CL-CLIP, a CLIP-based COD framework that equips open-vocabulary detectors with better continual learning ability through cost-volume-guided category decoupling. Specifically, following CAT-Seg, we compute a CLIP image-text similarity cost volume, defined as dense category-wise response maps between visual tokens and class text embeddings. This zero-shot spatial prior decomposes shared region features into class-specific pathways, which are then processed by a Multi-Expert RoI head. Extensive experiments on PASCAL VOC and MS-COCO show that CL-CLIP substantially improves the F-ViT baseline under continual fine-tuning and achieves competitive performance with existing continual object detectors, especially in adapting to newly introduced categories while preserving competitive base-class performance.

item.title: Sparse Orthogonal Parameters Tuning for Continual Learning
item.dc:creator: Kun-Peng Ning
Hai-Jian Ke
Yu-Yang Liu
Jia-Yu Yao
Yong-Hong Tian
Li Yuan
item.description: Continual learning methods based on pre-trained models (PTMs) have recently gained attention which adapt to successive downstream tasks without catastrophic forgetting. It typically refrains from updating the pre-trained parameters and employs additional adapters, prompts, and classifiers. However, these methods introduce additional complexity and fail to exploit the inherent properties of PTMs fully. In this paper, we propose Sparse Orthogonal Parameters TUning (SoTU), a novel parameter-efficient tuning method that leverages sparse delta parameters with orthogonality constraints to enhance continual learning. Through extensive empirical analysis, we observe that merging high-sparsity delta parameters across tasks can significantly mitigate forgetting, whereas low sparsity leads to severe parameter conflicts. We theoretically analyzed that high-sparsity delta parameters can preserve task-specific knowledge while maintaining performance akin to pre-training. Guided by this insight, SoTU enforces sparsity and approximate orthogonality in parameter updates, ensuring that knowledge from different tasks remains mutually independent yet synergistic. Furthermore, we propose a lightweight merging strategy to integrate delta parameters efficiently without requiring additional classifiers or complex architectures. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse CL benchmarks demonstrate that SoTU significantly outperforms existing CL methods, achieving optimal feature representation for streaming data without necessitating complex classifier designs. Notably, SoTU enables parameter-efficient continual adaptation while preserving PTMs integrity, making it a scalable and effective solution for real-world CL applications. Published: 2026-06-08T19:33:21+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Computer Vision Score: 0.773 (must_read)

Authors: Kun-Peng Ning; Hai-Jian Ke; Yu-Yang Liu; Jia-Yu Yao; Yong-Hong Tian; Li Yuan

Journal: International Journal of Computer Vision

DOI: 10.1007/s11263-026-02902-3

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Continual learning methods based on pre-trained models (PTMs) have recently gained attention which adapt to successive downstream tasks without catastrophic forgetting. It typically refrains from updating the pre-trained parameters and employs additional adapters, prompts, and classifiers. However, these methods introduce additional complexity and fail to exploit the inherent properties of PTMs fully. In this paper, we propose Sparse Orthogonal Parameters TUning (SoTU), a novel parameter-efficient tuning method that leverages sparse delta parameters with orthogonality constraints to enhance continual learning. Through extensive empirical analysis, we observe that merging high-sparsity delta parameters across tasks can significantly mitigate forgetting, whereas low sparsity leads to severe parameter conflicts. We theoretically analyzed that high-sparsity delta parameters can preserve task-specific knowledge while maintaining performance akin to pre-training. Guided by this insight, SoTU enforces sparsity and approximate orthogonality in parameter updates, ensuring that knowledge from different tasks remains mutually independent yet synergistic. Furthermore, we propose a lightweight merging strategy to integrate delta parameters efficiently without requiring additional classifiers or complex architectures. Comprehensive evaluations on diverse CL benchmarks demonstrate that SoTU significantly outperforms existing CL methods, achieving optimal feature representation for streaming data without necessitating complex classifier designs. Notably, SoTU enables parameter-efficient continual adaptation while preserving PTMs integrity, making it a scalable and effective solution for real-world CL applications.

item.title: TLight: A Triple-Modality Benchmark for Extreme Low-light Enhancement
item.dc:creator: Shaonan Liu
Guoshun Nan
Leisen Niu
Ke Xu
Xiaofeng Tao
Xudong Jiang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
item.description: Extreme Low-Light Enhancement (ELLE) aims to improve the visibility and quality of images captured under very low-illuminance conditions, typically below 0.1 lux. Existing learning-based methods commonly learn a direct mapping from a low-light image to a clean image. However, these methods struggle to recover visual details when they are overwhelmed by noise. Intuitively, the near-infrared image is robust to illumination variations such that is able to provide more structural cues under extremely low-illuminance conditions. Motivated by this, we propose TLight, a triple-modality (infrared-RAW-RGB) benchmark for RAW-domain ELLE. Our TLight consists of 300 indoor and outdoor scenarios, with each scenario comprising a triplet of three images: 1) a low-light RAW image as the input, 2) a normal-light RGB as the groundtruth and 3) an infrared image as additional input that is invariant to lighting conditions to provide supplementary structural cues. On top of our TLight, we develop a dual-fusion network (DFN) with a dual-branch encoder to exploit the complementary information in RAW and infrared images. Following DFN, a trainable ISP module is constructed to mimic the traditional ISP pipeline to obtain the final result. Extensive experimental results validate the effectiveness of our method, which achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on diverse benchmark datasets while maintaining competitive efficiency. Published: 2026-06-08T19:54:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology Score: 0.773 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shaonan Liu; Guoshun Nan; Leisen Niu; Ke Xu; Xiaofeng Tao; Xudong Jiang

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology

DOI: 10.1109/tcsvt.2026.3701028

Relevance: 0.773 (must_read)

Extreme Low-Light Enhancement (ELLE) aims to improve the visibility and quality of images captured under very low-illuminance conditions, typically below 0.1 lux. Existing learning-based methods commonly learn a direct mapping from a low-light image to a clean image. However, these methods struggle to recover visual details when they are overwhelmed by noise. Intuitively, the near-infrared image is robust to illumination variations such that is able to provide more structural cues under extremely low-illuminance conditions. Motivated by this, we propose TLight, a triple-modality (infrared-RAW-RGB) benchmark for RAW-domain ELLE. Our TLight consists of 300 indoor and outdoor scenarios, with each scenario comprising a triplet of three images: 1) a low-light RAW image as the input, 2) a normal-light RGB as the groundtruth and 3) an infrared image as additional input that is invariant to lighting conditions to provide supplementary structural cues. On top of our TLight, we develop a dual-fusion network (DFN) with a dual-branch encoder to exploit the complementary information in RAW and infrared images. Following DFN, a trainable ISP module is constructed to mimic the traditional ISP pipeline to obtain the final result. Extensive experimental results validate the effectiveness of our method, which achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on diverse benchmark datasets while maintaining competitive efficiency.
